problem_summary: 2016 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability/environmental): build a metric to measure water scarcity, apply it to a chosen region, project ~15 years forward, and design intervention strategies. This team (Control #43443, O-award) created a per-capita "Excess Water Ratio" (EWR) metric as an improvement over the existing Water Stress Indicator (WSI), applied it as a case study to India, projected its EWR to 2031 (and out to when EWR hits zero, ~2083), and evaluated supply-side and demand-side interventions — concluding that infrastructure interventions barely move the needle (delay zero-EWR by 1 year) while a 30-year crop shift from rice/wheat to millet delays it by ~14 years (2083 to 2097).
models_used: Excess Water Ratio (EWR): a novel per-capita scarcity metric, EWR = (MAR - WU_industrial - WU_agricultural - WU_personal) / Population, built explicitly as an improvement on the Water Stress Indicator (WSI = Withdrawals / Mean Annual Runoff)

Algebraic re-expression of EWR in terms of WSI to bypass missing MAR data: EWR = (1/WSI - 1)*(withdrawals/population) — used because India's national MAR was not directly available but WSI was published

Component-decomposition growth model: each water-use sector decomposed into output x water-footprint (Industrial = O_i*F_i, Agricultural = O_a*F_a, Domestic = U*W_u + R*W_r), then projected with compound/exponential growth using the sum-of-growth-rates approximation g(product) ~ g(factor1)+g(factor2)
Population-weighted domestic growth rate: g(D) = (U*W_u/D)*g(U) + (R*W_r/D)*g(R), splitting urban vs rural cohorts (computed g(D)=0.0122)
Exponential projection of total water use to 2031: WaterUse = I(1+g(I))^t + A(1+g(A))^t + P(1+g(D))^t, yielding 1306e9 m^3 by 2031

Polynomial interpolation model (MATLAB polyfit): fit polynomials to the Indian government's discrete water-availability/use predictions for 1997, 2010, 2025, 2050 to estimate EWR for intermediate years; expanded EWR with 7 water-use categories (agriculture, domestic, industrial, power, inland navigation, ecology, evaporation) minus WAPC

Intervention-impact accounting model: watershed development (each watershed ~276,800 m^3/yr for agriculture; 50 new ones -> 1.38e7 m^3/yr), wastewater treatment (treat the 11.03e9 m^3/yr currently untreated via ~651 new plants), and crop substitution to millet (rice 1250mm vs wheat 550mm vs millet 350mm; saves 900mm per rice-unit, additional 200mm per wheat-unit, phased over 30 years)
More-complex EWR variant adding imports and environmental flow: EWR = (MAR + Import - WU_i - WU_a - WU_p - WU_e)/Population, incorporating ecological water needs WU_e
key_techniques: Anchoring a novel metric to an authoritative incumbent (WSI used by UN Environment Programme) for credibility, then articulating one sharp differentiator: per-capita normalization, which the WSI lacks

Validation by correlation: plotting EWR vs WSI across 10 countries (Argentina, Colombia, China, India, Russia, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Spain, Sweden, USA) to show the new metric tracks the established one while adding resolution (e.g. China and USA have near-identical WSI 0.478/0.499 but China's EWR is ~3x smaller)
Algebraic substitution to route around a data gap — rewriting EWR using WSI when MAR was unavailable at national scale

Worst-case framing: deliberately treating sectoral growth as exponential and footprints as constant, then explicitly labeling the result a lower bound / worst case, which makes an aggressive prediction defensible rather than naive

Two-tier modeling: a transparent hand-computed 'preliminary' growth model first, then a 'more robust' MATLAB polyfit model grounded in official government projections — giving both intuition and rigor
Quantifying every intervention into the SAME EWR units (m^3/yr added or saved) so heterogeneous policies (watersheds, treatment plants, crop swaps) become directly comparable on one curve
Counterfactual comparison via the year-EWR-hits-zero as a single headline outcome metric (2083 baseline -> 2084 with infrastructure -> 2097 with millet), making the supply-vs-demand conclusion vivid
Supply-side vs demand-side taxonomy borrowed from the FAO framework to organize interventions and justify why demand-side wins
Honest sensitivity reasoning: noting the model is more pessimistic than the Indian government's and explaining exactly why (exponential assumption, constant footprints)
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on the dimensions O-awards reward: (1) Genuine modeling originality — it does not just apply WSI, it invents EWR with a clear, defensible rationale (per-capita impact) and validates it against the incumbent, which is exactly the 'develop a metric' ask of Problem E. (2) Decision-relevance — the central insight that supply-side fixes are nearly useless (1-year delay) while demand-side crop change buys 14 years is a counterintuitive, actionable, quantified conclusion that answers the real question rather than producing numbers for their own sake. (3) Resourcefulness under data scarcity — the WSI-substitution trick and the use of the government's own discrete projections with polyfit show mathematical maturity and pragmatism. (4) Intellectual honesty — it consistently frames its aggressive output as a worst-case lower bound and openly states it is more pessimistic than official forecasts, which builds judge trust. (5) Strong supply-and-demand framing tied to a credible literature (FAO, World Resources Institute, AQUASTAT) makes the policy section evidence-based, not hand-waved.
section_highlights: The Summary Sheet is a model of its kind: it states the metric, the method, the case, and the punchline numbers (EWR halves by 2031; infrastructure delays zero-EWR by only 1 year, 2083->2084; millet substitution pushes it to 2097) in one paragraph — a judge knows the whole story before page 2. The Introduction earns its length with a memorable scarcity framing (usable water is 0.003% of freshwater; population timeline from 1 to 7.3 billion). Section 2 cleanly separates metric definition, assumptions (five explicit, each justified), the EWR formula, and an Evaluation subsection with an explicit strengths/weaknesses list AND a correlation table validating against WSI — this validate-your-own-metric move is the structural standout. The India case study front-loads 'Main Drivers' (rainfall variability, poor irrigation, groundwater overdraft, contamination) before modeling, grounding the math in real causes. The intervention section is organized supply-side vs demand-side and gives each intervention its own quantified water contribution plus a candid strengths/weaknesses pass (e.g. watershed development historically harmed women's grazing income; wastewater raises downstream salinity). Figures 1-4 carry the argument visually with vs-without-intervention EWR curves.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The headline projections rest on stacked optimistic-then-pessimistic assumptions: constant MAR over 15 years (admittedly ignores climate change), constant industrial/agricultural water footprints (g(F)=0), and purely exponential sector growth — the team flags these but the long-range extrapolations to 2083/2097 push a model calibrated on near-term data far beyond its validity

The two models are not reconciled: the preliminary growth model predicts India runs out of water BEFORE 2031, while the polyfit/government-based model shows EWR merely halving by 2031 and surviving to ~2083 — a large discrepancy that is acknowledged but never resolved into a single coherent forecast

The figure data shown is suspiciously linear/identical across upper and lower bounds (Figures 1, 3, 4 lower=upper), suggesting the bound-spread and some curve detail may be schematic rather than computed

Geographic/temporal distribution is assumed away (a stated assumption), yet the paper itself identifies spatial-temporal rainfall variability as India's #1 scarcity driver — the model cannot capture the very problem it diagnoses
EWR treats agriculture, industry, personal as monolithic blocks (the team admits this), so it cannot model intra-sector efficiency gains that are central to real water policy

The millet intervention assumes all rice+wheat converts to millet over 30 years with simple per-unit mm savings, ignoring yield/caloric-area differences and the demand/cultural barriers the paper itself raises
Some quantitative leaps are thinly sourced (e.g. plant build times borrowed from US analogues, single per-hectare water figures applied nationwide)
reusable_patterns: Metric-improvement play: take the field-standard index the problem implies, name ONE concrete weakness (here: no per-capita view), define a new metric fixing it, then validate it by correlation/scatter against the incumbent across many cases. Directly transferable to any 'design a measure' MCM/ICM prompt.

Data-gap algebra: when a needed raw quantity (MAR) is unavailable but a published composite (WSI) contains it, algebraically re-express your formula in terms of the available quantity instead of guessing the missing one.

Two-tier model pairing: present a transparent closed-form/hand-computed model for intuition AND a computational model (polyfit, simulation) grounded in authoritative data for rigor — covers both 'show your reasoning' and 'show your sophistication'.
Common-unit intervention comparison: convert every proposed policy into the same output unit as your core metric (m^3/yr here) so heterogeneous interventions plot on one curve and rank objectively.
Single headline counterfactual metric: pick one vivid scalar (year-the-resource-hits-zero) and report it for baseline vs each intervention — turns a complex model into a one-line decision insight.

Worst-case lower-bound framing: when forced into aggressive assumptions, explicitly declare the result a bound and note how it compares to authoritative forecasts — converts a weakness into a credibility asset.
Supply-vs-demand (or any dual-lever) taxonomy from a domain authority (FAO) to structure the intervention analysis and motivate which lever dominates.
Per-section strengths/weaknesses honesty pass, including social/equity side effects (women's grazing land, downstream salinity), which signals maturity to judges.
Summary sheet that delivers metric + method + case + numeric punchlines in one paragraph so the whole result is legible before the body.
